content.problemStatement:
Vishal loves running. He often visits his favourite Nehru Park and runs for very long distances. On one such visit he found that the number of girls in the park was unusually high. Now he wants to use this as an opportunity to impress a large number of girls with his awesome speed.

The track on which he runs is an N\text{N}N kilometres long straight path. There are ai\mathbf{a_i}ai​ girls standing within the ith\text{ith}ith kilometre of this path. A girl will be impressed only if Vishal is running at his maximum speed when he passes by her. But he can run at his best speed only for a single continuous stretch of K\text{K}K kilometres. Now Vishal wants to know what is the maximum number of girls that he can impress.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two space-separated integers N and K (1 ≤ K ≤ N), where N is the length of the track in kilometres. The second line contains N space-separated integers a1, a2, …, aN, where ai denotes the number of girls standing in the i-th kilometre (1 ≤ ai ≤ 100).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible number of girls Vishal can impress by running at his maximum speed for exactly K consecutive kilometres.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The best 2‑kilometre segment is kilometres 2‑3 (4+8), giving 12 impressed girls.
The segment kilometres 2‑4 (2+3+4) yields the maximum sum 9.
The last 5 kilometres (6+7+8+9+10) give the largest possible sum 40.

solution.approach: The number of impressed girls for a chosen K‑kilometre stretch is the sum of the ai values inside that stretch. We need the maximum sum over all contiguous subarrays of length K. This is a classic sliding‑window problem: compute the sum of the first K elements, then slide the window one position at a time, updating the sum by adding the new element and subtracting the element that leaves the window. Keep the best sum seen. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input array.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        K = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        cur = sum(a[:K])
        best = cur
        for i in range(K, N):
            cur += a[i] - a[i - K]
            if cur > best:
                best = cur
        out_lines.append(str(best))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
